summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEEDURE – Instructions for Expert Witnesses – Where the parties propose that different material be provided to a Single Joint Expert – Where the applicant and Independent Children’s Lawyer concur on the material to be provided – Where the respondent seeks to include 822 pages of further documentation – Where the Court finds that the material sought to be included by the respondent is self-serving, irrelevant or likely to cause unwarranted delay or cost consequences – Where the Court dictates a list of material to be provided to the Single Joint Expert.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALTOBELLI J:

BACKGROUND

This matter came before me yesterday afternoon on an urgent basis to determine a dispute between the applicant mother (“the mother”) and the respondent father (“the father”) (collectively “the parents”) and the Independent Children's Lawyer as to the terms of a letter to a Single Joint Expert. The substantive matter involves both alteration of property interests and parenting orders in relation to X born in 2017; who is eight years old, lives with the mother and spends time with the father. The matter is set down for final hearing over five days commencing 17 August 2026. This case has all the hallmarks of a high conflict dispute, but what is not clear, because the evidence has not been tested by cross-examination, is whether the conflict is asymmetrical in nature, meaning, is the conflict being caused by one party more than the other.

BOUNDARIES OF THE CURRENT DISPUTE

For present purposes there are two relevant documents. The first is the draft letter to Dr K (“the Single Joint Expert”) dated 12 February 2026 prepared by the Independent Children’s Lawyer and agreed to by the mother. This document will become an Annexure A to these reasons for judgment. The focus of the dispute is the documents to be forwarded to the Single Joint Expert. Of significance, is that item 17 is no longer pressed, and items 22 and 23 are not in dispute.
The father contends that the documents he objects to, items 18–21, are dated and would not assist the Single Joint Expert to better understand X’s post-treatment health. The Court accepts that the Single Joint Expert would be assisted by the most current evidence about X’s health. But, for the time being, the documents proposed by the Independent Children’s Lawyer and the mother seem to be what is available. The documents respond to a concern that I raised in my most recent judgment in this matter (Austin & Banks [2026] FedCFamC1F 73). I do not accept the father’s concerns about the inclusion of these documents, and thus they will be so included.
The second relevant document is the draft letter to the Single Joint Expert dated 16 March 2026, prepared by the father, who is currently representing himself. He has made an application for representation under s 102NA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). This letter will become Annexure B to these reasons.
For present purposes, the focus will be on specifically items 10–11 and 15–18 of Annexure B. These are the documents that the father would like to include in the letter to the Single Joint Expert but which the Independent Children’s Lawyer and the mother oppose. The parties submitted tender bundles of significance, and as I pointed out to them, the father; in effect, proposes that 822 further pages be provided to the Single Joint Expert as compared to the mother and the Independent Children’s Lawyer.
The focus of this decision turns to the further documents the father proposes to include.

APPLICABLE LAW

This dispute will be determined by reference to rr 7.04 and 7.05 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”). These rules refer to and incorporate the ‘Overarching Purpose Rule’; r 1.04, whilst r 7.02 sets out the purpose of Part seven of the Rules. The exact text of the abovementioned rules provide as follows:
1.04 Overarching purpose
(1) The overarching purpose of these Rules, as provided by section 67 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court Act, is to facilitate the just resolution of disputes according to law and as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible.
Note 1: These Rules must be interpreted and applied, and any power conferred or duty imposed by them must be exercised or carried out, in the way that best promotes the overarching purpose (see subsection 67(3) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court Act).

Note 2: See sections 190 and 191 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court Act in relation to the overarching purpose of the Rules of the Federal Circuit and Family Court (Division 2). See also the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) (Family Law) Rules 2021 which applies these Rules with modifications.
(2) Parties to family law proceedings must conduct the proceeding (including negotiations for settlement of the dispute to which the proceeding relates) in a way that is consistent with the overarching purpose.
Note: See subsection 68(1) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court Act.
(3) A party’s lawyer must, in the conduct of a proceeding before the court (including negotiations for settlement) on the party’s behalf:
(a) take account of the duty imposed on the party referred to in subrule (2); and
(b) assist the party to comply with the duty.

Note: See subsection 68(2) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court Act.
...

7.02 Purpose of Part 7.1

The purpose of this Part is as follows:
(a) to ensure that parties obtain expert evidence only in relation to a significant issue in dispute;

(b) to restrict expert evidence to that which is necessary to resolve or determine a proceeding;

(c) to ensure that, if practicable and without compromising the interests of justice, expert evidence is given on an issue by a single expert witness;

(d) to avoid unnecessary costs arising from the appointment of more than one expert witness;

(e) to enable a party to apply for permission to tender a report or adduce evidence from an expert witness appointed by that party, if that is necessary in the interests of justice.

...

7.04 Order for single expert witness
(1) The court may, on application or on its own initiative, order that expert evidence be given by a single expert witness.

(2) When considering whether to make an order under subrule (1), the court may take into account any matters relevant to making the order, which may include the following (without limiting the matters which may be relevant):

(a) the overarching purpose of these Rules (see rule 1.04) and the purpose of this Part (see rule 7.02);
(b) whether expert evidence on a particular issue is necessary;

(c) the nature of the issue in dispute;
(d) whether the issue falls within a substantially established area of knowledge;

(e) whether it is necessary for the court to have a range of opinion.
(3) The court may appoint a person as a single expert witness only if the person consents to the appointment.

(4) A party does not need the court’s permission to tender a report or adduce evidence from a single expert witness appointed under subrule (1).

...

7.05 Orders the court may make

The court may, in relation to the appointment of, instruction of, or conduct of a proceeding involving, a single expert witness make an order, including an order:
(a) requiring the parties to confer for the purpose of agreeing on the person to be appointed as a single expert witness; or

(b) that, if the parties cannot agree on who should be the single expert witness, the parties give the court a list stating:
(i) the names of people who are experts on the relevant issue and have consented to being appointed as an expert witness; and

(ii) the fee each expert will accept for preparing a report and attending court to give evidence; or

(c) appointing a single expert witness from the list prepared by the parties or in some other way; or

(d) determining any issue in dispute between the parties to ensure that clear instructions are given to the expert; or

(e) that the parties:
(i) confer for the purpose of preparing an agreed letter of instructions to the expert; and
(ii) submit a draft letter of instructions for settling by the court; or
(f) settling the instructions to be given to the expert; or
(g) authorising and giving instructions about any inspection, test or experiment to be carried out for the purposes of the report; or

(h) that a report not be released to a person or that access to the report be restricted

Suffice it to say that the focus of these rules is the just resolution of disputes according to law and as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible. Expert evidence is to be restricted to that which is necessary to resolve or determine a proceeding. Unnecessary costs must be avoided.

CONSIDERATION

Doing the best the Court can to understand the rationale of the father in seeking to provide the Single Joint Expert with further documents, it seems to be that he wishes the Single Joint Expert to understand his perspective and the efforts that he has undertaken to foster a relationship with, and to spend time with X. This, it must be recognised, occurs in a context where his application for the Single Joint Expert to be, in effect, recused was unsuccessful for reasons previously given by this Court. It is simply unnecessary for the Single Joint Expert to have available to her all of the material that is filed in these proceedings. It adds cost. Experience indicates that provision of excess documents tend to delay the preparation of any Single Joint Expert Report. Experience further indicates, moreover, that there is a risk of confusion and distraction by voluminous and unnecessary, or marginally necessary, material.
The father conceded in his submissions that there is some overlap between the documents that he seeks to be included and those that are already included, but this overlap was not particularised. The father agreed that some of the material he sought to include in the brief to the Single Joint Expert related to property. He sought to justify this on the basis that the Single Joint Expert in her earlier reports had expressly referred to the property part of the case. In fact, in the Single Joint Expert Report dated 4 February 2025 property is referred to a few times, but in general; not specific terms, and generally by way of background. However, in the Addendum Single Joint Expert Report dated 16 April 2025, the only specific reference to the property dispute is at paragraphs 24.1 and 24.2. In the latter, the Single Joint Expert says:
The dispute about money, and delay in resolving their financial interests is a way in which Mr. [Austin] continues to harass and exert control over Ms. [Banks]. The sooner this dispute is resolved, the more the parents are separated from each other’s affairs and they can start to live independent lives.
It is understandable if the father feels aggrieved by these peripheral comments by the Single Joint Expert, but that doesn’t open the door for the father to provide further background material about the property dispute, as that is not relevant to the role of the Single Joint Expert. The issue raised by the Single Joint Expert above is not about property proceedings per se, but rather a broader issue that she adverts to, namely harassment and control. The father is representing himself and thus may not fully appreciate that he is able to cross-examine the Single Joint Expert at the hearing about these matters. if they are relevant. In addition, after any further Single Joint Expert Report is received, there may be scope to issue a request for further information or to ask clarifying questions. But, on the material before the Court, it is hard to discern how it is relevant for the Single Joint Expert to have any evidence about the property dispute between the parties.

DETERMINATION

Having regard to the specific documents the father contends should be included, the Court cannot see the relevance of, or how the Court or the Single Joint Expert might be assisted by, the Single Joint Expert having the documents listed at items 10, 11, 15 and 16 of Annexure B.
Item 17 of Annexure B is an affidavit of the father filed 14 March 2026, ostensibly for the specific purpose of providing the father’s narrative to the Single Joint Expert. This affidavit was not an affidavit required for the purposes of the proceedings. It was not ordered or directed. On behalf of the mother, it is contended that it was self-serving. It probably is in terms of context. The father will have the opportunity to tell the Single Joint Expert what he wants at an interview. This further affidavit is inappropriate.
Item 18 of Annexure B is a Case Outline Document of the father’s filed 16 March 2026. It is not evidence, rather it is a submission and should not be included in the letter to the Single Joint Expert.
Accordingly, a letter should be sent to the Single Joint Expert in terms of the Annexure A to these reasons, with the exception of item 17. I note in passing that the references to ‘Family Report’ on page two paragraph three, and possibly thereafter, should be amended to reflect that this is a Single Joint Expert Report, not a Family Report. The letter should also contain the new contact details of the father who is representing himself for now.

CONCLUSION

I encourage the parties to consider the cost implications of repeatedly bringing this case back before the Court. I note that the father will hopefully receive the benefit of representation under s 102NA of the Act. I remind the father in these reasons for judgment that if, for whatever reason, that representation does not come to fruition, the matter may proceed in the absence of legal representation, and the father will be precluded from cross-examining the mother at the final hearing.
